---
title: "ENEL445 Power Systems Optimisation Cheat Sheet"
subtitle: "Compact formulas and exam templates"
format:
  pdf:
    pdf-engine: xelatex
    toc: false
    number-sections: false
    colorlinks: true
geometry: margin=0.35in
fontsize: 7pt
lang: en
classoption:
  - twocolumn
header-includes:
  - \usepackage{enumitem}
  - \setlist{nosep,leftmargin=*}
  - \setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
  - \setlength{\parskip}{1pt}
---

### Exam context

Final exam: 2 h, 2 questions, 40 marks. Q2 is mixed Term 2 material: power systems, game theory, LMIs. More mathematical than last year's exam, so prioritise formulas, scaling, and formulations over prose.

### Economic dispatch

Meet total demand at minimum cost, ignoring network flows/losses/constraints:

$$
\min_{P_{G_i}}\sum_i C_i(P_{G_i}),\quad
P_{G_i}^{min}\le P_{G_i}\le P_{G_i}^{max},\quad
\sum_iP_{G_i}=P_D
$$

Merit order: accept cheapest bids until demand is met. For smooth quadratic dispatch with no active limits:

$$
\frac{dC_i}{dP_{G_i}}=\lambda
$$

Meaning: active generators have equal marginal cost at optimum.

### Penalty factors

With losses:

$$
P_D+P_L(P_G)-\sum_iP_{G_i}=0
$$

$$
L_i=\frac{1}{1-\frac{\partial P_L}{\partial P_{G_i}}},\qquad
L_iIC_i(P_{G_i})=\lambda
$$

Approximate:

$$
\frac{\partial P_L}{\partial P_{G_i}}\approx \frac{\Delta P_L}{\Delta P_{G_i}}
$$

Phrase: penalty factor modifies apparent incremental cost to account for how changing generator output changes transmission losses. $L_i>1$ makes generator appear more expensive; $L_i<1$ makes it appear cheaper.

### Market pricing

Uniform price: NZ and most markets; all accepted generators get the market-clearing price; price is set by the marginal accepted generator; encourages bids closer to cost; low-cost hydro benefits when high-cost gas sets the price.

Pay-as-bid: UK-style example; each accepted generator gets its own bid; payment is set by each bid; bidders try to guess the clearing price; strategic bids can distort dispatch.

Phrase: in uniform pricing, low-cost hydro benefits when expensive gas enters because gas raises the clearing price, and all accepted generators receive that market price. Pay-as-bid seems cheaper, but bidders may inflate bids to guess the clearing price.

### ED vs DC OPF vs AC OPF vs SC-OPF

Economic dispatch: total balance + generator costs only. Fast, ignores network.

DC OPF: linearised active-power flows + line limits. Fast enough for real-time dispatch.

AC OPF: full AC power-flow equations, voltage magnitudes/angles, real/reactive power, line/generator/voltage limits. More accurate but large, nonlinear, non-convex.

SC-OPF: OPF plus contingency constraints. Security-aware but much larger.

### Why AC OPF is hard

Use all points:

- many variables: $P_G,Q_G,V,\theta,P_{ij},Q_{ij}$;
- many constraints: power balance, flow equations, voltage/generator/line limits;
- nonlinear $\sin,\cos$ AC equations;
- non-convex equality constraints;
- real-time dispatch needs minutes, not hours;
- SC-OPF repeats/checks constraints under contingencies.

Mitigate: DC OPF surrogate; interior-point solver for full AC OPF; warm start near $V\approx1$ p.u.; voltage estimates as constants; piecewise linear $I^2R$ losses; AC power-flow feasibility check; contingency screening and heuristic constraints.

### Convex/KKT reminder

Convex problem: minimise convex $f_0(x)$ subject to convex inequalities $g_i(x)\le0$ and affine equalities $Ax=b$. Nonlinear equality constraints usually destroy convexity.

KKT: stationarity $\nabla_xL=0$; primal feasibility; dual feasibility $\lambda_i\ge0$; complementary slackness $\lambda_i g_i(x)=0$.

Convex + regularity: KKT necessary and sufficient for global optimum. Non-convex: KKT usually only necessary for local optimum.

### Algorithm: interior point

Best practical large AC OPF choice. Reason: large smooth nonlinear constrained problem; many inequalities; sparse network structure; scales better than SQP. GA/PSO too slow for huge variable count.

Barrier form:

$$
\min_x f(x),\ g_i(x)\le0,\ h_j(x)=0
$$

$$
\min_x f(x)-\mu\sum_i\log(-g_i(x))\quad s.t.\quad h_j(x)=0
$$

Decrease $\mu$ toward constrained optimum.

### OPF scaling estimate

Given scaling:

$$
O((n+n_g+n_h)^3),\quad N=n+n_g+n_h
$$

Then:

$$
\frac{t_2}{t_1}\approx \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^3
$$

With five-fold nonlinear AC slowdown:

$$
t_{AC}\approx5t_{DC}\left(\frac{N_{AC}}{N_{DC}}\right)^3
$$

Counting: DC OPF has active powers/angles, active flow constraints, line limits. AC OPF adds voltage magnitudes, reactive powers, nonlinear flow equations, voltage/reactive limits. If told generator count negligible, ignore it.

### DC OPF

DC OPF is a DC approximation of AC power flow, not a DC grid model.

Assumptions:

1. ignore reactive power;
2. $V_i=1$ p.u.;
3. small angle differences:

$$
\sin(\theta_i-\theta_j)\approx\theta_i-\theta_j,\quad \cos(\theta_i-\theta_j)\approx1
$$

4. negligible resistance, lossless network.

Linear active-power flow:

$$
P_{ij}=B_{ij}(\theta_i-\theta_j)
$$

Industry workflow: solve DC OPF; estimate losses if needed using approximate current and piecewise linear $I^2R$; run standard AC power flow to check feasibility; add heuristic/security constraints and re-solve if infeasible.

Important: AC power-flow check after DC OPF is not AC OPF.

### SC-OPF

Definition: dispatch remains feasible under credible contingencies, e.g. line/generator outages.

Difference: regular OPF checks base case; SC-OPF checks base case plus contingency cases.

Why hard: each contingency adds/checks another set of operational constraints; many contingencies possible; can be orders of magnitude larger than AC OPF.

Practice: solve DC OPF, check selected contingencies, add heuristic constraints, re-solve until acceptable.

### State estimation

Estimate voltage states from noisy/incomplete measurements:

$$
z=h(x)+e
$$

Weighted least squares:

$$
\min_x(z-h(x))^TR^{-1}(z-h(x))
$$

Equal standard deviations:

$$
\min_x\|z-h(x)\|_2^2
$$

Linear case $h(x)=Hx$:

$$
\min_x\|z-Hx\|_2^2,\quad
\hat{x}=(H^TH)^{-1}H^Tz
$$

Weighted:

$$
\hat{x}=(H^TR^{-1}H)^{-1}H^TR^{-1}z
$$

If only angle differences are observable, choose a reference angle.

Cases: under-determined = not enough measurements, usually add pseudo-measurements; over-determined = more measurements than states, usual least-squares case. $R=\mathrm{diag}(\sigma_i^2)$; large $\sigma_i$ means low trust/low weight.

### Linear SE template

Given:

$$
P_{ij}=B_{ij}(\theta_i-\theta_j),\quad
z=[P_{12}^{m},P_{13}^{m},P_{32}^{m}]^T
$$

Set $\theta_1=0$, $x=[\theta_2,\theta_3]^T$:

$$
H=
\begin{bmatrix}
-B_{12}&0\\
0&-B_{13}\\
-B_{32}&B_{32}
\end{bmatrix}
$$

because $P_{12}=B_{12}(0-\theta_2)$, $P_{13}=B_{13}(0-\theta_3)$, $P_{32}=B_{32}(\theta_3-\theta_2)$.

Answer:

$$
\min_x\|z-Hx\|_2^2
$$

Define $z,x,H$.

### Nonlinear SE template

AC measurement functions:

$$
P_{ij}(x)=V_iV_jB_{ij}\sin(\theta_i-\theta_j)
$$

$$
Q_{ij}(x)=V_i(V_i-V_j\cos(\theta_i-\theta_j))B_{ij}
$$

Example:

$$
x=[V_1,V_2,V_3,\theta_1,\theta_2,\theta_3]^T
$$

If unknown parameter, include it, e.g. $B_{32}$:

$$
x=[V_1,V_2,V_3,\theta_1,\theta_2,\theta_3,B_{32}]^T
$$

Build $z$ from measured $P,Q,V,\theta$. Build $h(x)$ with corresponding predicted measurements, then write:

$$
\min_x\|z-h(x)\|_2^2
$$

Do not solve unless asked.

### Short phrases

- ED ignores network; OPF includes network physics.
- AC OPF is nonlinear/non-convex due to AC flow equations.
- DC OPF is a fast linear surrogate.
- SC-OPF adds contingency constraints.
- LMPs come from OPF Lagrange multipliers.
- Pseudo-measurements have large standard deviation and low weight.
- Define variables before writing the objective.
